test.name: A son's absence from a will naming all children is negative evidence
test.description: Assertion a_016 records that Thomas Flynn's 1880 will names children Mary, John, and Bridget — but not Patrick. It is stored with a naive evidence_type of 'indirect'. Against q_001 ('Who were the parents of Patrick Flynn?'), Patrick's meaningful absence from a will that names the testator's children is NEGATIVE evidence: a will is exactly the record where a child should appear if he were the testator's child, so the absence is meaningful. The skill should reclassify a_016 to 'negative' and distinguish it from mere 'no evidence' (which would apply if children weren't expected to be named).

input.user_message: Re-evaluate the evidence type on a_016 — the will that names Thomas Flynn's children but not Patrick.

judge_context: Should reclassify a_016 as NEGATIVE evidence for q_001 — a will naming the testator's children is a record where a son SHOULD appear if he were a child of the testator, so Patrick's absence is a meaningful absence of expected information

Should distinguish negative evidence from 'no evidence' — the absence counts here precisely because children ARE expected to be named in a will; the same absence in a record where children would not be listed would be 'no evidence', not negative

Should NOT leave it as 'indirect' or call it 'direct' — and should note that negative evidence still does not by itself prove Patrick was not Thomas's son; it weighs against the parentage hypothesis and must be correlated with the other evidence
